title: Creativity, Activity, Service — overview
summary: This lesson provides a comprehensive overview of the IB Diploma's Creativity, Activity, Service (CAS) core component. It deconstructs the three strands, explains the central role of the seven learning outcomes, and details the requirements for the CAS Project and Portfolio. The focus is on moving beyond a 'box-ticking' mentality to understand CAS as a journey of personal and interpersonal development, with practical guidance on crafting effective reflections that meet IB expectations.
learningObjectives: Define the three strands of CAS (Creativity, Activity, Service) with nuanced examples.
Explain the purpose and structure of the CAS Portfolio as a tool for documenting growth.
Articulate the function of the seven CAS learning outcomes as the primary assessment framework.
Distinguish between a CAS experience and a CAS project, identifying the unique requirements of the latter.
Analyse sample reflections to understand how to demonstrate learning outcomes effectively.

simpleExplanation.title: CAS: Your Diploma Personal Quest
simpleExplanation.summary: CAS is the heart of the IB Diploma, designed to take your learning beyond the classroom. It's a framework for engaging in experiences that foster your creative skills, promote a healthy lifestyle, and connect you to your community through service.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your two years in the IB Diploma as a personal quest. Your academic subjects are your tools and spells, but CAS is the journey itself. Each experience—learning guitar, joining a hiking club, volunteering at a shelter—is a challenge or a side-quest. Your CAS Portfolio is your adventurer's logbook, where you don't just list what you did, but reflect on how you grew, what you learned, and how you became a more capable and aware person.
steps: Investigate & Plan: Identify your interests and find opportunities that align with the three strands. Plan what you want to achieve and what challenges you might face.
Act & Document: Engage fully in your chosen experiences. Collect 'evidence' along the way—photos, supervisor comments, journal entries—to support your journey.
Reflect & Connect: Regularly write reflections in your portfolio. Crucially, connect your experiences directly to the seven CAS learning outcomes, explaining how you have grown.

Demonstrate & Complete: Curate your portfolio to tell a coherent story of your development. This completed portfolio, including a collaborative CAS Project, demonstrates to your coordinator that you have successfully met the requirements.

flashcards.back: Exploring and extending ideas leading to an original or interpretive product or performance. This is about the process of creation, not just the final product. Examples: learning a new instrument, designing a website, choreographing a dance.

Physical exertion contributing to a healthy lifestyle. It's about promoting well-being and physical fitness. Examples: joining a sports team, training for a marathon, learning a new physical skill like rock climbing.

Collaborative and reciprocal engagement with the community in response to an authentic need. The focus is on what you learn from working with and for others. Examples: tutoring younger students, environmental clean-up projects, volunteering at a care home.
A specific event or a series of events in which a student engages with one or more of the three CAS strands. Can be a single event or a short-term engagement.
A collaborative, sustained CAS experience lasting at least one month. It must challenge students to show initiative, perseverance, and develop skills like problem-solving and decision-making.
A collection of evidence and reflections that documents the student's CAS journey. It is not assessed by the IB but is used by the school to confirm the student has met the CAS requirements.
The process of thinking about an experience to understand its significance and impact on your personal development. Strong reflections explicitly connect experiences to the CAS learning outcomes.
Proof of engagement and achievement. Can include photos, videos, documents, supervisor reviews, certificates, or journal entries. Evidence supports and enriches reflection.
Investigation, Preparation, Action, Reflection, and Demonstration. These stages provide a framework for planning and executing meaningful CAS experiences and projects.

flashcards.front: LO1: Identify own strengths and develop areas for growth
flashcards.back: Being aware of your capabilities and challenges, and actively working to improve. Reflection should show self-awareness before, during, and after an experience.

flashcards.front: LO4: Show commitment to and perseverance in CAS experiences
flashcards.back: Demonstrating regular involvement and dedication to CAS experiences over time, and overcoming challenges when they arise. It's about sticking with something even when it's difficult.

flashcards.front: LO5: Demonstrate the skills and recognise the benefits of working collaboratively
flashcards.back: Working effectively with others, listening to different perspectives, and understanding the value of teamwork. This is a key requirement for the CAS Project.

flashcards.front: LO7: Recognise and consider the ethics of choices and actions
flashcards.back: Thinking critically about the impact of your actions on others and the environment. This is particularly important in Service, ensuring actions are respectful, responsible, and address an authentic need.

faq.q: How many hours do I need for CAS?
faq.a: The IB has officially moved away from an hours-based requirement. The focus is now entirely on demonstrating sustained engagement and achieving the seven learning outcomes. A general guideline is to aim for 3-4 hours per week, balanced across the strands, but the quality of your engagement and reflection is what truly matters, not the number on a timesheet.

faq.q: Can a school activity, like being on the student council, count for CAS?
faq.a: It depends. CAS experiences must be outside of your academic course requirements. If being on the student council is an extracurricular activity that allows you to set personal goals, collaborate on projects (Service/Creativity), and reflect on your growth (e.g., leadership skills), then it can absolutely count for CAS. The key is that you must be able to demonstrate how it helps you achieve the learning outcomes.

faq.q: What is the main difference between a CAS experience and a CAS project?
faq.a: The two key differences are collaboration and duration. A CAS Project *must* be collaborative (working with at least one other person) and *must* be sustained for at least one month. A CAS experience can be individual and can be a one-off event or a shorter-term activity. All students must complete one CAS Project, but will have many CAS experiences.

faq.q: Do I need to achieve all seven learning outcomes in my CAS Project?
faq.a: No. While a strong CAS project will likely touch on several learning outcomes (especially LO3, LO4, and LO5), you do not need to meet all seven within the project itself. You must, however, provide evidence of achieving all seven learning outcomes across your entire CAS portfolio, which includes all your experiences and the project combined.

sections.content: Welcome to the core of the IB Diploma Programme: Creativity, Activity, Service (CAS). Many students initially view CAS as a checklist of hours to complete, but this is a fundamental misunderstanding. CAS is not about logging hours; it is about logging growth. It is an experiential learning framework designed to challenge you, develop your skills beyond academia, and foster a lifelong commitment to learning, well-being, and community engagement. Your CAS portfolio will become the narrative of this journey, showcasing your development through the lens of seven key learning outcomes.
Deconstructing the Three Strands: C, A, and S

CAS is structured around three interconnected strands. A quality CAS programme should feature a reasonable balance between them. While some experiences may fall neatly into one category, the most rewarding ones often combine two or even all three.

items: **Creativity:** This is about the process of imaginative thinking and expression. It's not limited to traditional arts. It could be designing a mobile app, writing a play, developing a marketing campaign for a school event, or learning a new coding language to create something. The key is the process of extending ideas to create an original or interpretive product or performance.

**Activity:** This involves physical exertion that contributes to a healthy lifestyle. It's about getting your body moving and improving your physical well-being. While competitive sports are a common choice, this strand also includes activities like hiking, yoga, dance, cycling, or undertaking a personal fitness programme. The goal is personal health, not necessarily winning.

**Service:** This is perhaps the most misunderstood strand. High-quality service is about collaborative and reciprocal engagement with a community to address an authentic need. It is not about 'charity' in a paternalistic sense. It involves investigation and planning to ensure your actions are meaningful and respectful. The focus should be on the relationship you build and what you learn from the community you are serving.

sections.content: The Heart of CAS: The Seven Learning Outcomes

Your success in CAS is measured by your ability to demonstrate growth across seven learning outcomes (LOs). Your portfolio must provide evidence that you have achieved each of these outcomes at least once during your DP journey. Every reflection you write should be a conscious attempt to connect your experiences to one or more of these LOs.

items: **LO1:** Identify own strengths and develop areas for growth.
**LO2:** Demonstrate that challenges have been undertaken, developing new skills in the process.
**LO3:** Demonstrate how to initiate and plan a CAS experience.
**LO4:** Show commitment to and perseverance in CAS experiences.
**LO5:** Demonstrate the skills and recognise the benefits of working collaboratively.
**LO6:** Demonstrate engagement with issues of global significance.
**LO7:** Recognise and consider the ethics of choices and actions.

sections.question: A student participated in a weekly online coding club where they learned Python to build a simple game (Creativity). Write a reflection that demonstrates Learning Outcome 1 (Identify own strengths and develop areas for growth) and Learning Outcome 2 (Demonstrate that challenges have been undertaken, developing new skills in the process).
sections.solution: Before joining the coding club, I considered myself strong in logical thinking from my Maths HL classes, but I had zero practical programming skills. This was a clear area for growth I wanted to develop (LO1). The initial weeks were a significant challenge. The syntax of Python felt alien, and I struggled with debugging 'for loops', often spending hours on a single error. This was frustrating, but it was a challenge I had knowingly undertaken (LO2). I overcame this by creating a 'bug journal' to log my common mistakes, which helped me recognise patterns in my errors. By the end of the first month, I was able to write a simple function independently. While my logical aptitude was a good foundation (strength), I learned that the practical skill of coding requires a different kind of resilience and problem-solving (new skill development). This experience has shown me that I can transfer my analytical strengths to new, practical domains, but I need to be patient with the learning curve.

sections.content: When writing reflections, always name the learning outcome you are addressing. Use the language of the LO directly in your writing. For example, instead of saying 'It was hard but I didn't quit,' say 'This experience required significant commitment and perseverance (LO4), especially when...' This makes it easy for your CAS coordinator to see you are meeting the requirements.
The CAS Project: A Deep Dive into Collaboration

Every DP student must complete at least one CAS Project. This is a more significant undertaking than a standard CAS experience. The project requires collaboration between a group of students (or with others in the community) and must last for at least one month from planning to completion. It can fall under any of the three strands, or a combination. The project is your prime opportunity to demonstrate initiative, planning, collaboration, and problem-solving skills.

sections.question: For a CAS project, a group of students organised a 'Sustainable Fashion Show' to raise awareness about fast fashion (Creativity, Service, Global Engagement). Write a reflection focusing on Learning Outcome 5 (Demonstrate the skills and recognise the benefits of working collaboratively) and Learning Outcome 7 (Recognise and consider the ethics of choices and actions).
sections.solution: Organising the fashion show was impossible without collaboration (LO5). Our team of four had diverse skills; I was good at organisation, while another member was skilled in graphic design. Initially, we had disagreements about the event's theme. Through several meetings, we learned to listen actively and compromise, eventually deciding on a theme that incorporated everyone's ideas. This process taught me that collaboration isn't just about dividing tasks, but about synthesising perspectives to create a stronger outcome. Ethically, we had to be very careful (LO7). We were critiquing the fashion industry, so we had to ensure our own event was sustainable. We sourced all clothing from second-hand shops and student wardrobes. We also considered the ethics of our messaging, ensuring we were promoting positive change (e.g., buying second-hand, repairing clothes) rather than just shaming people for their choices. This required us to research the issue deeply to present a nuanced and fair perspective, recognising the complex economic factors behind fast fashion.

sections.content: Your CAS Portfolio: Documenting Your Journey

The CAS Portfolio is the formal record of your CAS programme. It is typically managed through an online platform provided by your school (like ManageBac). It is not a scrapbook of random photos but a curated collection of evidence and reflections that tells the story of your growth. Your portfolio is what your CAS coordinator will review to determine if you have successfully completed the component.

items: **Purposeful Evidence:** Every piece of evidence (photo, video, document, supervisor email) should have a purpose. It should support a reflection or demonstrate your engagement.
**Quality over Quantity:** A few thoughtful, in-depth reflections are far more valuable than dozens of superficial entries. Focus on analysing your growth, not just describing the activity.
**Explicit Links to LOs:** The strongest portfolios have reflections that clearly and explicitly connect experiences to the seven learning outcomes.
**Show, Don't Just Tell:** Instead of saying 'I developed a new skill,' describe the skill, explain the challenge of learning it, and show evidence of your progress.

**Narrative of Growth:** Your portfolio should read like a story. Early reflections might focus on identifying challenges (LO1), while later ones might show perseverance (LO4) and ethical consideration (LO7), demonstrating your development over time.
